ADS decouples logit shift into architecture dependency and data dependency, revealing that higher ADS is associated with a larger logit shift after training on new tasks. The framework, based on three mechanistic components, shows a strong monotonic correlation with logit shift across over 175 diverse architectures.

items.title: Detect by Yourself: Self-Designing Agentic Workflows for Few-Shot Graph Anomaly Detection
items.summary: Self-designing agentic workflows for few-shot graph anomaly detection (SignGAD) propose a novel paradigm to reformulate graph anomaly detection from fixed pipelines to task-conditioned detection workflows. SignGAD selects suitable graph encodings and detector designs to exploit task-specific anomaly evidence, and introduces a guarded final refit strategy to refine the selected workflow under limited supervision. This approach achieves strong performance against state-of-the-art methods on several real-world datasets.

items.title: HEAL: Resilient and Self-* Hub-based Learning
items.summary: Researchers have introduced a novel decentralized learning framework called HEAL (Resilient and Self-* Hub-based Learning). HEAL combines the strengths of Federated Learning, Gossip Learning, and Epidemic Learning to promote dynamically chosen nodes as aggregators through an optimized self-organizing and self-healing P2P overlay. Through simulations, HEAL demonstrates similar performance to Federated Learning in crash-free settings, outperforming Gossip and Epidemic Learning in crash and churn prone environments.

items.title: Calibrated Inference for the Conditional Average Treatment Effect in the Few-Placebo Regime via Gaussian Processes
items.summary: Estimating the conditional average treatment effect (CATE) is crucial in decision-making fields, but standard methods can produce unreliable calibrated uncertainty intervals due to bias in nuisance models fitted to smaller treatment arms. A new method, GP-CATE, proposes using Gaussian processes to model each arm's outcome surface, directly incorporating the scarce arm's uncertainty into the posterior. This approach yields calibrated coverage across synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks, outperforming other estimators like Causal Forest and BART in terms of reliability.

items.title: Stop Suppressing the Tail: Causal Inference for Extreme Events
items.summary: Estimating how an outcome responds to a continuous treatment is a core causal-inference primitive. However, standard robust double machine learning deliberately suppresses extreme events due to heavy tails, omitting 1-in-1000 events that are actual target quantities in high-stakes settings. A proposed estimator emits a structured tail-shape output and evaluates the per-treatment tail shape, successfully breaking circular dependence and rendering diagnostics invariant to core method choice. The estimator reduces deep-tail return-level MAE by 11% and conditional-shortfall MAE by 25.5%.

items.title: Iterative Causal Discovery: Per-Edge Impossibility Certificates, Tier-Aware Oracle Queries, and the $1+K$ Lower Bound
items.summary: Causal-discovery algorithms return a directed graph without providing principled means to distinguish edge directions from those assigned without identifying assumptions. To address this, a protocol attaches a discrete impossibility certificate to each candidate edge, distinguishing between resolved and impossible orientations. A bivariate cascade with gated identifiability tiers is used to identify recoverable DAGs through expert interactions, establishing an upper bound of $1+K$ expert interactions sufficient to recover any DAG.

items.title: Triangular-Reference Schr\"odinger Bridges for Time Series Generation
items.summary: A new method called Triangular-Reference Schrödinger Bridges for Time Series (TR-SBTS) extends the SBTS framework by using an intervalwise frozen diffusion reference that is triangular across multiple latent volatility levels. The variational core of SBTS remains intact, with the entropy minimizer being the h-transform of the reference. TR-SBTS preserves stability and convergence of regularized kernel estimators through a finite-dimensional conditioning map combining three complementary reductions and a coupled state-covariance bridge step.

items.title: Identifiable Bayesian Deep Generative Copulas with Unknown Layer Widths for Data with Arbitrary Marginal Distributions
items.summary: A new deep generative model called the Deep Discrete Encoder (DDE) Copula is introduced for analyzing multivariate data with arbitrary marginal distributions. The model uses a hierarchical directed network of binary latent variables inside a copula framework to enable flexible dependence modeling for mixed discrete and continuous data. Estimation is based on rank likelihoods, which decouple marginal modeling from posterior inference on the DDE parameters, allowing identification of the model parameters under certain conditions.

items.title: Semiparametrically Efficient Inference for Kernel Measures of Noise Heterogeneity
items.summary: Researchers developed semiparametrically efficient inference for analyzing noise heterogeneity in additive noise models using kernel measures. This framework addresses the issue of first-stage bias inherited from regression error, which can lead to spurious dependence between covariates and residuals. The novel estimator yields bootstrap-calibrated tests for residual independence and goodness of fit, as well as asymptotically efficient confidence intervals for the kernel dependence measure.

items.title: Accelerating Reinforcement Learning Training Using Simulation Surrogate Models
items.summary: High-fidelity simulation models are used to analyze complex stochastic systems due to their high computational cost, leading to the development of cheaper surrogate models. Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful framework for making online decisions in stochastic environments, often utilizing simulation models as training environments. Numerical experiments on a stochastic service system demonstrated that leveraging surrogate models can substantially accelerate RL training and re-training.

items.title: Evolving and Detecting Multi-Turn Deception using Geometric Signatures
items.summary: A unified pipeline generates realistic multi-turn deceptive question sets via genetic prompt optimization with co-evolving mutation operators. Researchers detected deceptive attempts using simple, explainable geometric signals in embedding space coupled with a lightweight feed-forward classifier. The model achieved consistently high recall and test-time F1 across base, reworded, and truncated scenarios. Geometric features such as angular coverage, distance ratio, and linearity augmented with pairwise similarity statistics led to a compact predictive model.

items.title: Unsupervised Identification and Removal of Spurious Correlations During Fine-Tuning
items.summary: Fine-tuning a pretrained language model can produce spurious correlations between the fine-tuning task and unintended latent factors that lead to bias and reduced generalization. To address this, researchers propose GRASP, which removes spurious correlations from the weights of a naive LoRA fine-tune without removing the underlying latent factor. This approach aims to preserve any genuine task signal while preventing new reliance on identified latent patterns. GRASP achieves significant improvements in misalignment reduction and financial task performance in three experimentally verified tasks.

items.title: Soft Specialists: $\alpha$-R\'enyi Ensembles for Uncertainty-Aware LLM Post-Training
items.summary: Existing large language models learn from heterogeneous, conflicting data by compressing uncertainties into a single pattern of behavior. An $\alpha$-R\'{e}nyi variational framework offers an uncertainty-aware alternative to deep ensembles, interpolating between classical Bayes and predictive posterior learning. This approach provides a scalable training procedure for supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization, enabling model specialization and actionable uncertainty estimates across tasks. Local stability criteria demonstrate how model misspecification can manifest contradictory data as epistemic uncertainty.

items.title: A Factory-Floor Deployment Case Study of VLA Pipelines for Industrial Packaging Task: Workflow, Failures, and Lessons
items.summary: A deployment study was conducted at Siemens Factory in Germany using Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies for industrial packaging tasks. The robot was trained on 2535 episodes (10 hours) of data from factory settings, with a pipeline consisting of repeated loops of data collection, curation, fine-tuning, evaluation, and targeted recovery data collection. This deployment demonstrated recurring failure modes and provided lessons to improve the deployment workflow.

items.title: GE-Sim 2.0: A Roadmap Towards Comprehensive Closed-loop Video World Simulators for Robotic Manipulation
items.summary: GE-Sim 2.0 is a closed-loop video world simulator for robotic manipulation that improves action-following fidelity and trajectory coverage by re-training on thousands of hours of real-world robot data. The simulator features three new modules: a state expert, a world judge, and an acceleration framework. GE-Sim 2.0 tops the public WorldArena leaderboard at only 2B parameters, outperforming dedicated robotic world models and general video generators.

items.title: SCALE-COMM: Shared, Contrastively-Aligned Latent Embeddings for MARL Communication
items.summary: SCALE-COMM proposes a self-supervised framework for learning compact, stable, and policy-relevant communication representations in decentralized MARL settings. The approach decouples communication learning from policy optimization by training low-dimensional latent messages that capture task-relevant planning and traffic information. SCALE-COMM consistently outperforms existing communication frameworks across standard MARL benchmarks and a realistic warehouse coordination task. The learned communication space yields improved stability, sample efficiency, and throughput under policy fine-tuning.

items.title: Inducing Calmness With Pocket-Sized Robotics: Reducing Movement and Heart Rate in Children through Hand-Held Tactile Interactions
items.summary: Research found that interaction with a pocket-sized tactile device reduced children's heart rate by 3.56 bpm and overall movement by 38%. The device used in the study was a hand-held rhythmic vibration-matching game designed to focus attention and encourage stillness, leading to increased calmness and better behavior regulation. This simple technology may provide a means of promoting calmness and self-regulation in children. Tactile interaction reduced physiological arousal and physical restlessness in typically developing children.

items.title: Synthetic Emotions vs. Gamification: Exploring Engagement Strategies for Small Social Robots in Different Age Groups
items.summary: Children with anxiety disorders often struggle with emotional regulation and social interactions, making socially assistive robots effective only if they remain engaged. A tactile robot was evaluated for its ability to engage children aged 6-8 years and university students aged 20-27 years through synthetic emotional feedback or point rewards. While school children preferred emotional engagement, university students achieved higher task accuracy with points-based systems that sustained performance over time. The findings highlight the importance of validating design assumptions through observed interaction.

items.title: Uni-LaViRA: Language-Vision-Robot Actions Translation for Unified Embodied Navigation
items.summary: Uni-LaViRA presents a unified agentic architecture for embodied navigation by translating language, vision, and robot actions into a single decision structure. The task can be reasoned about using pretrained multimodal large language models, allowing for zero-shot learning on four task families and four real robots in a heterogeneous environment. Two agent-loop mechanisms, Memory (TDM) and Second Chance Backtrack (SCB), enable practical unification of the approach. Uni-LaViRA achieves state-of-the-art results without training effort, outperforming recent navigation foundation models that require millions of samples and GPU-hours.

items.title: Agentic Language-to-Objective Synthesis for Optofluidic Assembly
items.summary: Researchers have introduced Speak-to-Objective, a modular agentic pipeline that uses a Large Language Model to translate human commands into executable operations for assembling microparticles. The pipeline separates intent from action by generating descriptive objectives in an actuator-agnostic fashion and learning from user feedback. Using laser-induced thermoviscous flows as a physical actuation modality, the system demonstrates natural-language-programmable microscale assembly of particle patterns in a microfluidic environment.

items.title: Trinity: Unifying Class-Agnostic Terrain and Semantic Segmentation for Unstructured Outdoor Environments by Leveraging Synthetic Data
items.summary: Researchers propose a transformer-based architecture called Trinity that jointly performs class-specific semantic segmentation and class-agnostic terrain segmentation within a unified network. This formulation enables the learning of robot-agnostic visual terrain priors for downstream tasks such as traversability estimation and mission planning. A new synthetic dataset, RUGDSynth, and an existing dataset, EXTerra, are introduced to enable large-scale training with diverse terrain appearances. The approach demonstrates feasibility in complex outdoor environments through experiments.

items.title: Design of a Real-time Asynchronous Monocular Odometry for Planetary Exploration
items.summary: A preliminary design for a real-time asynchronous event-based monocular odometry has been proposed for planetary exploration. Event cameras are used to report pixel-wise brightness changes at microsecond resolution, addressing high-speed sensing and HDR lighting needs. An Error-State Kalman Filter (ESKF) is leveraged to continuously estimate camera ego-motion from the asynchronous event stream. The camera state is updated with every tracked position output generated by RATE, a real-time asynchronous feature tracker.

items.title: Simulation-Informed Diffusion for Decentralized Multi-robot Motion Planning
items.summary: Decentralized multi-robot motion planning requires each robot to generate collision-free trajectories without global sensing or reliable communication. Simulation-Informed Diffusion (SID) overcomes this challenge by using constraint-aware diffusion models to simulate neighboring robots' future trajectories, enabling a minimal communication scheme that triggers coordination only when necessary in congested scenarios. SID consistently outperforms baseline methods in terms of planning effectiveness and constraint satisfaction. It scales to scenarios with 108 robots and 160 obstacles.
